title: Part 22
text: to Liminal Politics: Towards an Anthropology of Political Revolutions 211 Girard, once a process of undifferentiation unfolds, the process of doubling threatens to spread, and it can only be brought to a halt via sacrifice. In the last years of his life, Turner (e.g., 1988: 34) came to recognize the importance of Girard, and in the precise context of the ritual structure: crisis is contagious, like a plague, and sometimes the ‘redressive machinery ... fails to function’, leading to ‘a reversion to crisis’ (ibid.: 35). These reflections help us to understand crucial aspects of modern revolutions that are normally not addressed. More problematic is the dominant idea in most comparative approaches to revolutions, that they happen when a sufficient number of individuals make a rational cost-benefit calculation that they can gain from a revolution (and they calculate that the gains are greater than the risks). This does not take into account the liminal setting in which most people are forced to make choices. Human action in liminality poses particular challenges that cannot be understood through a rational choice vocabulary: when pushed to the limit by the force of events, humans simply cannot take structures for granted. The notion of ‘interest’ or ‘rational action’ is made obsolete the moment there are no background structures or certainties against which to weigh such interests. That is also why people in such situations will search for models that they can follow or imitate. Another point concerns the paradoxical mixture of uncontrolled emotionality and utmost rationality, expressed in cool, legal, Enlightenment language. Modern political revolutions are far from that rational movement dedicated to recreating justice and equality that revolutionaries invoke to tell their own story. Or rather, they are indeed such rational movements, but they contain an equal element of the carnivalesque setting loose of forces. 11 Turner’s discussion of the contagious nature of crisis also helps us to understand how violence is often difficult to tame once revolutionary processes begin to unfold. The restoration of peace itself is difficult, since it must happen via a reestablishment of viable relations between the contending partners, or a public recognition of an irreparable schism. But this does not always happen. As Turner says, very often the schism produced becomes fatal and enduring (1988: 104). This relates to the question of outcomes, and the evident fact, systematically obscured in our schoolbooks, that most often modern revolutions, far from providing freedom and rights, lead to more state centralization, and very often to more violence, of the clearly Puritan type. The point was perhaps best noted by Mumford in his analysis of the Baroque city: it was ‘[t]hrough the very workings of democracy [that] baroque absolutism tightened its hold upon society’ (quoted in Szakolczai 2000: 183). As Eisenstadt always stressed, the Jacobin elements of the French Revolution are an inherent part of modernity, and belong to the core of our revolutionary tradition, and hence they cannot be cast aside as an unhappy 11 Revolutions here resemble the public spectacles produced by the chain gangs throughout France until the early nineteenth century. This festival of departing convicts, as Foucault clearly saw, was a ‘festival of fools, in which the reversal of roles is practised’ (1979: 259). Liminality and the Modern 212 consequence of otherwise noble principles. Finally, this almost systematic outbreak of internal violence will often take on an outward dimension, propelling the revolutionary movement and the singled out enemies into external warfare, all still in playful combinations of the rational and the emotionally volatile. The meaningful timeframe for studying the French Revolution might not be 1789–1991, or even 1789–1799; it must somehow take into account events as they unfolded between 1789 and 1815, including total war and the destruction and near collapse of the whole of Western civilization. The Bolsheviks did, after all, have a model to imitate. But the obvious question is whether these logics can be confined to communism or are not rather, as Girard would suggest to us, an inherent feature of modernity, even in its liberal forms. Revolutions and the Liminal Origins of Political Modernity We have argued that the study of political revolutions ought to figure more prominently in both ethnography and anthropological theory. Put briefly, anthropology has much to offer political science and political sociology precisely in the context of the study of political revolutions and what Turner called ‘macropolitics’. Revolutions more than any other event in modern history represent those instants of ‘pure potentiality’ that Turner invoked (1969: 41), moments in which given hierarchies, social norms and sacred values are brought into question. Human action and human experience during such events take on a new importance. One can indeed talk about an anthropological approach, linked to Sewell’s notion of event-history (1995), an approach to some extent anticipated by historically oriented anthropologists (Knudsen 1989). This links the study of revolutions to anthropological approaches to drama and ritual, and to the larger performative turn in the social sciences, with and beyond Victor Turner. It is from such a perspective that it suddenly becomes clear why the slogans of the French Revolution might as well have been heard shouted by a cohort of Ndembu neophytes: liberty, equality, fraternity. This chapter has not argued that anthropology can provide political scientists with the missing piece of information that can help them complete the picture. Rather, we have to give real space to a situation of contingency, uncertain outcomes and limited knowledge. Liminality, as Bauman said (1994: 15), is inherently ambivalent. In liminality, Turner echoes, ambiguity reigns (1988: 102). This implies that causal explanations and structuralist frameworks have their limits. In fact – and it is important to stress this – liminality is not a concept that could ever explain anything. But this statement has a value in itself: political theorists have tried for four ‘generations’ now to come up with the perfect model for understanding revolutions, but to invoke liminality is to recognize that there is no perfect model. It is not possible to establish a general model that would enable us to explain and predict when and where, and under what exact conditions, revolutions have occurred and will occur. As argued by Turner, events Liminal Politics: Towards an Anthropology of Political Revolutions 213 and performances are not simply structured, but have their own life. We have to study such moments as real instances of contingency, moments where meaning- formation and symbolism condense and take new forms. That is far from saying that there are no recognizable patterns in political revolutions. In fact, the van Gennep-Turner framework proposed here does indicate shared patterns as well as shared dangers and problematics playing out in revolutionary processes. We would like to support the revolutions in the Arab world as a step toward more democracy, freedom and justice. We tend to forget that they are directed against political leaderships that were or are themselves fashioned on mimetic experiments of Western revolutionary traditions, calling for freedom, brotherhood and justice. In their national forms that emerged in the postcolonial setting, they merely gave new ideological impetus and fervour to what was already an inherent feature of the modern world. It is not easy to make discernments and take positions in the current situation: power structures are complex and available models for political emancipation all seem overburdened. But what seems clear enough is that we need better ethnographic accounts of revolutions in their unfolding. Revolutions serve as zero points of history, dramatic moments of foundation for the most diverse political systems around the globe, including Iran, America, Russia, Egypt, France, China, England, Cuba and Libya. Both liberalism and socialism are founded on revolutionary appeals and display in their differing idioms what one might term a ‘revolutionary epistemology’. This indicates that we have to disentangle the study of revolutions from their connected ideologies and make comparisons at the level of ritualization and symbolism. It also indicates that we somehow still have to come to terms with the fact that our modern world is inherently built on a series of revolutions, liminal origins to which we problematically return. This represents a foundational aspect of political modernity that anthropological tools can help to throw a critical light on. And it is this liminal aspect of modernity that can now be fully explicated. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 9 By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality The ritual sequence, or rather, the processual, playful order that Arnold van Gennep detected and named in his study of cultural ceremonies, contains at the centre, in the middle of everything, a phase and space of liminality. This was not something that van Gennep invented or a theory he ‘constructed’. It was something he literally dis-covered; it was already there, and Marcel Mauss could have done better than to ridicule van Gennep’s ground-breaking work for ‘stating the obvious’. If there is one reason that we still read Mauss’ work on gift-giving today, then it is not only for its honest and engaged use of the existing ethnographic record for a comparative purpose: it is precisely this ‘A-ha!’ sensation that it brings to the reader in an almost timeless way. It says something so simple and evident that we simply have to give in and realize that giving, receiving and giving back lies at the heart of meaningful sociability. Everywhere. Arnold van Gennep’s dis-covery of the tripartite structure of rituals became the ordering device of his immense life’s work. It was an illumination for himself. After his death, and with the English publication of Rites of Passage, it also became his belated claim to fame. Most people who know the name Arnold van Gennep know him for that one idea. It brought attention to the highly dynamic and transformative aspects of ritual which were wholly absent from the framework of Durkheim. This is one main reason for which Arnold van Gennep must be repositioned, and without any ‘but’, as a founding figure in the study not only of religion, but also of social life and social change in the most general way, far more useful than the conceptual and methodological apparatus set up by Durkheim. Edmund Leach and his British colleagues discovered this half a century ago and were quite explicit about it. Contrary to what one might have expected, this did not lead to any thorough revisiting of the life and work of Arnold van Gennep. Sociologists still today do not consider him. This might still change, especially now that we are no longer under the spell of any of those -isms that paradigmatically came to dominate the sciences throughout the twentieth century. Arnold van Gennep is the thinker of liminality. His own life was much stamped by critical threshold experiences, and also by his failed rite of passage into institutionalized academia. Van Gennep is without doubt the most published social scientist in France who failed to secure a job in a university. He eventually managed to throw off his bitterness, walk through another door, and give himself a new frame of work and purpose in life through his almost heroic, single-handed building up of French folklore. Van Gennep introduced the concept of liminality, and he analysed it with reference to a vast array of cultural rituals and practices. However, he was acutely Liminality and the Modern 216 aware that rituals, precisely in order to display an order, require closure and an act of reintegration. Liminality has its place within a sequence. No culture would be foolish enough to devise a ritual sequence that stops at the liminal. Without a return to normality and background structures that one can take for granted (at least until they are shaken again), individuals go crazy and societies become pathological. Human life ceases to be meaningful in perpetual liminality. And yet, there is an extent to which this is exactly what happened in one specific transition, the one that lies at the heart of social theorizing: the transition to modernity. Liminality and the Times in which We Live What has been argued throughout this book is that the contemporary world has problematically incorporated liminality at its core. It has been proposed that the anthropological concept of liminality serves to throw critical light on a series of phenomena which are normally not analysed as part of the same reality. Such an analysis of the liminal characteristics can be pushed in many different directions. We have offered just some examples here. By way of conclusion it may be worth pointing towards other areas of contemporary life in which the concept of liminality shows its relevance as a diagnostic tool of the times in which we live. A proper substantiation of the claims made here must await future elaboration, but given the seriousness of the challenges we face, we risk these formulations. Globalization and Global Wars: The Loss of Boundaries Here one must quite obviously start with globalization. Cutting short the endless debates concerning a correct definition and theoretical explanation of the term, it is probably safe to say that globalization essentially has to do with the disappearance and breaking down of previously existing boundaries. This is visible already in the long series of terms used to pinpoint globalization as a process starting with a ‘de-’, from de-regulation to de-territorialization. Emblematically, the process of globalization was made possible by something that disappeared, a wall which was broken down and a boundary that was transgressed – and that from within the one city in Europe existing at the thresholds, in an institutionalized in-between position and geographic, political-economic limbo. For very good reasons, we celebrated this. But the optimism that characterized the early 1990s has slowly petered out; and in many ways some of the most problematic aspects of current globalization have to do with just such a continuous loss of limits. We have argued in Chapter 8 that revolutions – and to some extent the very vocabulary of political modernity – are quite evidently liminal at their core. At the level of politics, this relates to an understanding that also engages the liminal origins of democracy itself (Wydra 2009, Forlenza 2011). As these lines are being written, dramatic events in Syria and Egypt provide frightening evidence of what happens when the liminal period remains an open laboratory for self-declared By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 217 masters of ceremony and mass-produced mimetic violence; crisis is contagious, Turner said. These liminal features, alas, also involve the nature of contemporary international relations and global security politics. This analysis is currently being advanced by a handful of scholars working within international relations (see in particular Mälksoo 2012, 1 Yanik 2011). And there are indeed some extremely problematic ways in which the permanently liminal is at play also here. This became evident in the very naming of the ‘Global War on Terror’. Two essential features legitimized the claim to novelty here: that this war, in contrast to all previous ones, had no spatial or temporal boundaries. It is nowhere and everywhere, and it has no real end; it is boundless and essentially liminal. As Mälksoo states most explicitly, ‘Modern international conflict has turned the condition of perpetual liminality into a universal experience’ (2012: 492). The permanently liminal is visible also in the technologies of warfare. What could indicate this better than drones, these invisible robot-like 2 fighter planes hanging in the skies, ready to kill? We are told that this is our future ‘security paradigm’. One of the latest US drone programmes has been named the Gorgon Stare, evidently with reference to the Greek mythological figure whose gaze could turn victims into stone. These drone programs are not only killing people more efficiently. A primary goal of drone surveillance is to collect overhead imagery that will prove tactically useful. This quest for total knowledge amounts to global, total surveillance. This all forms part of what Wall and Monahan (2011) have aptly analysed as a permanently liminal, global security-space. This is not a question of taking sides; what America is doing now others will do tomorrow. And global terrorists are real enough. They are the contemporary exemplars of that figure identified by Marcel Mauss in his analysis of the Bolsheviks, namely the ‘professional revolutionary’. They may be organizing in novel types of networks and fleshing out their eschatological fantasies from a slightly different ideological core. Gnostic moderns they remain. And just like their predecessors, they expound an ideology which is purely abstract, based on empty slogans. Far from being faithful and self-effacing servants of the people, they are demagogues and adventurers, revelling in their return from exile, murky elements exploiting any opportunity to accumulate disorders and follies. They have no connection to and no genuine interest in the people they claim to fight for. While fancying themselves heirs to glorious revolutionary traditions, they are in fact mere imitators 1 The entire issue of Review of International Studies in which this article appeared was dedicated to a security-related discussion of liminality, with a foreword by Iver Neumann. 2 In robotics, great advances are currently made in producing killer machines, also operating from the ground and under water. It is worth remembering that the word robot was first introduced by the Czech writer Karel Čapek in his play Rossum’s Universal Robots, published in Prague in 1920. The science fiction play is about a factory producing artificial people. The robots seem happy to work for humans. Eventually this changes, and a hostile robot rebellion leads to the extinction of the human race. The play was first staged in 1921, and instantly made it from Central Europe to Broadway. Liminality and the Modern 218 of the ancient tyrants. They show no sign of care or commitment towards concrete, existential communities. Herein lies their globality and their permanent liminality. Liminal Economies It is generally agreed that our current, global world is mainly driven by the economy. The contemporary relevance for what is argued here would therefore need to indicate ways in which the implosion of liminal conditions is equally evident within the economic sphere. A central feature of economic globalization, in the form in which it started to spread from the end of the Cold War, is its indefiniteness and boundlessness. There is no limit in space or time; markets remain open 24 hours a day – indeed, even this notion of ‘24 hours a day’ is clearly an inept and outdated way of conceiving of it, as if it still operates with days and hours. It would be more correct to say that time has been dissolved. The global economy is driven by the movement of resources and assets, in constant projection against an equally mobile future horizon of expectations. The ‘surplus value’ that Marx had tried to predict has been lifted out of time and place and with no reference to the materiality of objects; it is generated in a void. Expectations eventually collapse and lead people into disarray, but new ones evidently keep forming from the debris. We are seemingly caught in this perpetual liminal game. Any political attempt to ‘regulate’ it runs against not just power politics and technical challenges, but also a resignatory attitude towards the enormity of the issue, slipping out of our hands. Did we say ‘resources’? Here lies another element of liminality: those assets that move around in constant flux are to a high degree no-things: calculated values on the basis of calculated values. In his discussion of liminality, Kieran Keohane perceptively captures some of the essential characteristics of this ‘libidinal economy’, and the ‘financialization’ he fittingly describes as social pathogenesis (Keohane 2009). The sheer fact that our current economy has been described as ‘casino capitalism’ (and prophetically so by Strange in 1986) evidences the ways in which liminal play permeates the entire economic sector, making up the slippery soil beneath its self-sustained rationality. It is also one of the most visible ways in which the carnivalesque and the abstract-rational generation of value actually have come together. Bubbles in the Air: Perfect Nothingness The most down-to-earth (if it is still here) expression that frames this liminal/ libidinal economy is the constant eruption of ‘bubble economies’. Such bubbles must quite literally be understood as fragile surface entities floating around in space, rising above us, creating growth, increasing consumption, growing themselves – until they burst and the liminal nothingness falls down upon us. All bubbles burst, as children know all too well. Then we start to blow another one. By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 219 This is not just a recent epidemic. Economic historians generally concur that the first major economic bubble grew and then burst in Amsterdam in the year 1637, known as the great ‘Tulip Mania of 1637’. Incidentally, this just one year before the casino became institutionalized (and the decision to actually open the Ridotto was with all likelihood taken in 1637, when gambling was spontaneously spreading in the streets during what was turning into the quasi-permanent carnival in Venice). It was also the same year that the first opera house opened, still in Venice, near the Rialto, that Descartes published Discourse on Method, and that a series of events (including the explosion of the Scottish rebellion against Charles I) paved the way to civil war and puritanist revolution in Britain, creating a legacy. Hobbes returned to England that year, witnessing king and parliament in heated struggle, and decided that life consists of fear and greed. The first English trade-voyage venture to China was launched, challenging Spanish–Portuguese hegemony. In America, new English Puritans launched a war against the Pequot Indians, anticipating the larger genocide to come. There is an extent to which we still live in the aftermath of 1637. The tulip-bubble event was popularized in 1841 by a book written by Charles Mackay, fittingly entitled Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds. At the peak of the tulip mania some single tulip bulbs sold for more than ten times the annual income of a skilled craftsman. Then of course the prices plummeted. The tulip bulb is, after all, a poisonous onion, and onions are curiously empty at their core. Economists are greatly at odds when it comes to explaining bubble economies – and some stubbornly deny their existence. Technically speaking, an economic bubble is defined as ‘trade in high volumes at prices that are considerably at variance with intrinsic values’. Prices and ‘values’ keep going up and up, based on expectations and mimetic desire, whereas the intrinsic value stays the same – or goes down. Large stock market trends often begin and end with periods of frenzied buying (bubbles) or selling (crashes). The bubble grows and grows, until it bursts. This might sound like an innocent children’s story; it is the story of the hard realities and social epidemics of our economy. There is an acute need for social scientists to turn their hands and minds back to this madness, for it is far too serious a business to leave just to economists. This engagement must happen without taking departure from any ideological position, and certainly without positing state-control as an alternative to market forces. The workings of our economy are extremely complex and technical, but there can be no doubt that the underlying mechanisms are social and deeply anthropological, in both the empirical and the philosophical sense. Interests and Passions and the In-Between It is a widespread assumption made by economists (and not a few social and political scientists) that human beings are fundamentally driven by the rational pursuit of the satisfaction of their interests. The very nature of such interests, and Liminality and the Modern 220 how they emerge, is something that most economists – quite consciously, and very explicitly – leave others to think about. Marxists endorse the notion of ‘objective interests’, transposing the subject of such interests from the individual to the social class – and from that position as well, the question is closed before we even start to formulate it: what do human beings want? Liberal economists argue that ‘ends’ cannot be theorized or normatively accounted for – they are simply a matter of taste, and about taste we cannot argue (see here Szakolczai’s discussion, 2013c: 34–5). At the level of political philosophy, it was this very position that was argued for so famously by John Rawls and applied to political liberalism: we cannot know about what people aim for, all we can do is to generate a just system of distribution, an abstract system of fairness and justice without any reference to values, a system that can be brought to function no matter which human beings are inserted into it (see Thomassen 2012e). Here we must start from a well-known, if often overlooked fact: that the notion of ‘interests’ stems from the two Latin words, ‘inter’ and ‘esse’. The term literally refers to that which lies between, or happens between, beings or being: it is a ‘being in between’ (Horvath 2013: 195; Wydra 2009). The notion of ‘self-interest’ is literally meaningless. This is because interests are never simply ‘given’, nor are they simply deducible by ‘inserting’ an abstractly modelled individual into a social situation with all the relevant variables under control. Following Girard, what we want, what we desire, happens between human beings. The value of an object, lies not in its intrinsic qualities, but in how that object is viewed by more than one subject. With respect to economic value, of for example stocks, such value emerges in a temporal in-betweenness, in the tension between present and future; and a decisive factor here involves the expectations that human beings (investors in particular) have, their projections of the future, their projections of other people’s projections, matched against other people’s projections of your projections – and so on, into the myriad of economic models that have tried to map this essentially very social, mimetic and unpredictable situation of in-betweenness of subjects in the in-betweenness of now and tomorrow. In fact, as Plato and later Girard argued, human desires are utterly and foundationally mimetic. It was this perspective that Gabriel Tarde took up so convincingly. Shortly before his death in 1904, Tarde published La Psychologie Economique (1902/3), and developed a perspective recently summed up by Latour and Lepinay (2009). For Tarde, the ‘interests’ that are the very subject matter of the modern economy are indeed very ‘passionate’. ‘Far from being objective, interests involve a state of passion, a tension and intention, not to be sought at the level of the hidden essence of a human being’ (Horvath 2013: 196). The question then becomes how such passions are mediated or consciously manipulated. As further discussed by Horvath, this was the question that Albert Hirschman (1997) famously took up in The Passions and the Interests. Hirschman argued that passions were considered subject to control by precisely that idiom of objectifiable ‘interests’ that modern economics takes as its point of departure. This did not happen in the abstract, or in single minds and their calculations: it happened as a By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 221 historical process, as part of the transition to modernity. Part I of Hirschman’s book was fittingly called How the Interests were called upon to Counteract the Passions. But why had passions become such a problem that they had to be ‘counteracted’? The answer given by Hirschman was simple: this was the historical situation of incessant religious wars, the liminal period of the seventeenth century discussed in Chapter 5. The behavourial foundations of capitalism cannot be disconnected from economic history, and economic history – the origins and foundations of capitalism – cannot be separated from political history. And underneath it all rests an anthropological dimension, what Weber called the level of Menschentum, the kind of human being through which the institutional levels operate. The question posed by Hirschman is quite Weberian in style: how is it that an activity formerly judged amoral would, at a given point in time, be considered morally right? When St Augustine listed the three most basic sins of ‘fallen man’, lust for money and possessions was one of them. Machiavelli and Hobbes would later declare that religious values could no longer tie individuals to righteousness; they could not tame the disorderly passions of men. Hirschman’s argument is that ‘interests’ became the tamers of passions. From the eighteenth century the concept of interest came to be generally accepted, and was also ethically ‘ennobled’. It was an important part of this ‘acceptance’ that the concept remained flexibly and loosely defined, but according to Hirschman that did not prevent it from performing two essential functions: predictability and constancy. Those functions were building blocks for both modern politics and modern economics. The love of money-making was ‘softened’ and normalized, accepted and then venerated. In the theory of Adam Smith, the pursuit of self-interest was eventually identified as the very key to prosperity. The development was equally political. In standard approaches to politics, democracy was to be conceived as a public arena where actors could argue and fight out their interests, and rationally so. In the writings of thinkers like Montesquieu, Sir James Stuart and John Millar (all discussed by Hirschman in Chapter 2 of The Passions and the Interests), the idea was developed that the by- products of interest (i.e. commerce and industry) would also serve to constrain authoritarian decision-making by the sovereign (Locatelli 2006), and therefore pave the way for political freedoms – a cornerstone of liberalism ever since. As summed up by Horvath (2013: 196), ‘the objectivation of ‘passionate interests’ into a rational and calculable, manageable and predictable form was considered as a solution to the religious and political crisis that otherwise proved intractable’. The essential problem is that the ‘stability’ involved here is not generated from within experienced life, but comes to function as an externally imposed ideology of the system. The system is cut off from human experience, even as it claims to be based on it. In fact, the entire world-view is inherently problematic because it fails to treat the anthropological dimension in any serious way. Following Tarde, any
